message.content:
Test file: src/game-of-life.spec.ts
Implementation file: src/game-of-life.ts
Passing tests: 9 (all tests now implemented and passing)
Recent Green phase: No code change — the negative-coordinates test passes via the existing string-key encoding.

This is the final refactor for the kata. Refactor the implementation while keeping all tests green. Do a final pass over the whole file for any remaining clarity or simplicity improvements.

EXPERIMENT MODE: Run autonomously, return when done.

attachment.content:
- green: TDD Green Phase
- red: TDD Red Phase
- test-list: TDD Test List Phase
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text:
PRE baseline established:
- Tests: 9/9 passing
- ESLint smells: 0 (clean)

Now the APP mass and McCabe measurements per function.

## PRE-Measurement: APP Mass + McCabe

**`toKey`** — template literal interpolation. Mass: bindings (x,y) 2 + invocation (template) ~1 = ~3. McCabe: 1.

**`fromKey`** — `split`, `map(Number)`, destructure. Mass: bindings 3, invocations (split, map, Number ref) ~4, constant "," 1 = ~9. McCabe: 1.

**`neighborsOf`** — `map`. Mass: bindings ~5, invocations (map, +,+) ~5, loop 5 = ~15. McCabe: 1.

**`candidateCells`** — `flatMap`, `map`, Set, spread. Mass: bindings ~4, invocations (flatMap, map, new Set, spread) ~8, loops (flatMap, map) 10 = ~22. McCabe: 1.

**`countLiveNeighbors`** — `filter`, `.length`, `has`, `toKey`. Mass: bindings ~4, invocations (filter, has, toKey) ~6, loop 5 = ~15. McCabe: 1.

**`isBorn`** — one comparison. Mass: bindings 1, constant ref 1, conditional (===) 4 = 6. McCabe: 1 (no branching keyword — `===` is an expression, not a decision point).

**`survives`** — `&&` plus `===`. Mass: bindings 2, conditional (===) 4, `&&` (1 logical) ~1 = ~7. McCabe: 2 (one `&&`).

**`survivesOrIsBorn`** — `||` joining two calls. Mass: bindings ~3, invocations (countLiveNeighbors, has, toKey, isBorn, survives) ~10, `||` ~1 = ~14. McCabe: 2 (one `||`).

**`nextGeneration`** — `new Set`, `map`, spread, `map`, `filter`. Mass: bindings ~3, invocations ~10, loops (map×3 / filter) ~15 = ~28. McCabe: 2 (the `||` lives in `survivesOrIsBorn`, but the filter callback adds no decision here).

**Worst McCabe:** all functions are at 1 or 2 — extremely flat. The decomposition is already clean: each function is small, single-purpose, and well-named.

## Naming Evaluation

- `toKey`/`fromKey` — clear serialization pair. Good.
- `neighborsOf`, `candidateCells`, `countLiveNeighbors` — reveal intent precisely. Good.
- `isBorn`, `survives`, `survivesOrIsBorn` — map directly to the rules. Good.
- `nextGeneration` — the public API name matches the kata. Good.

No naming improvements warranted.

## Refactoring Evaluation

The implementation is already at or near its floor on every metric:
- ESLint smells: 0
- McCabe: max of 2 across all functions
- Functions are tiny (2-6 lines), single-responsibility, composed cleanly
- The "set of live keys + candidate cells" approach is the idiomatic sparse representation

Let me look for one concrete improvement. In `survivesOrIsBorn`, `living.has(toKey(cell))` recomputes `toKey(cell)`, and `countLiveNeighbors` also computes keys. There's a subtle conceptual duplication: `isBorn` and `survives` both check counts against named constants, but the dispatch logic is fine. 

One small clarity nit: `isBorn` takes only `liveNeighbors` while `survives` takes `(isAlive, liveNeighbors)` — asymmetric signatures. I could try unifying, but that would couple two independent rules and reduce clarity (Rule 2 trumps APP/McCabe). Let me verify with the measurement discipline by attempting it.

Actually, the cleanest candidate: `fromKey` uses `.map(Number)` which produces `number[]`, then is returned as `Cell` (a tuple) — the destructure `const [x, y]` discards the tuple typing safety but works. This is fine.

Let me attempt one improvement — extracting the repeated `toKey(cell)` lookup pattern is not worth it. Given every metric is at its floor, I'll attempt the one change that could genuinely help readability: making the survival/birth rules symmetric and reading as the canonical Conway statement. Let me try it and measure.

input.description: Back up implementation before refactoring attempt

content.text: I'll attempt to consolidate the birth/survival rules into a single `isAliveNextGeneration` predicate that reads as the canonical Conway rule, removing the asymmetric `isBorn`/`survives` pair and the `survivesOrIsBorn` orchestrator.

content.text:
Tests still 9/9, ESLint still clean. Now I'll evaluate whether this change is actually an improvement by the measurements.

## POST-Measurement: APP Mass + McCabe

This change merged three functions (`isBorn`, `survives`, `survivesOrIsBorn`) into one (`isLiveNextGeneration`).

**Combined PRE mass** of the three functions: `isBorn` ~6 + `survives` ~7 + `survivesOrIsBorn` ~14 = ~27 (with two extra invocation hops for the `isBorn`/`survives` calls).

**`isLiveNextGeneration` POST**: bindings ~4, invocations (countLiveNeighbors, has, toKey) ~6, conditionals (two `===`) ~8, logicals (`||`, `&&`) ~2 = ~20.

- **APP mass (rule cluster)**: ~27 → ~20 (removed two invocation hops)
- **McCabe (the predicate)**: PRE `survivesOrIsBorn` = 2 → POST `isLiveNextGeneration` = 3 (now contains both the `||` and the `&&` that previously lived in separate functions). Total McCabe across file is conserved (2+2 in two fns → 3 in one fn, minus the trivial `isBorn` at 1).
- **Fewest elements (Rule 4)**: 3 functions → 1 function
- **ESLint smells**: 0 → 0
- **Cognitive complexity**: well under threshold either way

## Decision: Evaluate clarity (Rule 2 trumps APP/McCabe)

This is the key judgement. The pre-refactor version named each Conway rule explicitly (`isBorn`, `survives`) — that is highly intention-revealing and maps one-to-one onto the kata's specification rules. The merged version concentrates both rules into one boolean expression with named constants, which still reads clearly as "born by reproduction, OR alive and surviving" and matches the canonical statement of Conway's rule.

Both are defensible. The merged version wins on Rule 4 (fewest elements: 3→1) and APP mass (~27→~20) while keeping ESLint clean and tests green, and the named constants preserve intent within the single expression. The asymmetric `isBorn(n)` vs `survives(isAlive, n)` signatures are gone. I'll keep the change.

Let me do the final whole-file pass for any remaining clarity nits.

The file reads top-to-bottom as a clean pipeline: type → constants → key serialization → geometry → counting → rule → orchestration. No remaining clarity or simplicity nits:

- `neighborsOf` map callback returns `[x+dx, y+dy]` which TypeScript widens but is annotated `Cell[]` on the function — fine.
- `countLiveNeighbors` line-break before `.length` is an ESLint formatter artifact, not a smell (ESLint reports it clean).
- All names reveal intent; no duplication; minimal elements.

The implementation is at its floor. I'll clean up the backup and write the done marker.

content.text:
Refactoring complete. Final report below.

## Refactoring Complete

**Refactoring applied**: Merged the three rule functions (`isBorn`, `survives`, `survivesOrIsBorn`) into a single canonical predicate `isLiveNextGeneration`.

**Worst-offender selection**: All functions measured at McCabe 1–2 with zero ESLint smells — the surface was already at its floor. No naming change warranted (every name revealed intent). The only available lever was Rule 4 (fewest elements), targeting the asymmetric `isBorn(n)` / `survives(isAlive, n)` pair and their orchestrator.

**Pre/Post deltas (the rule cluster)**:
- ESLint smells: 0 → 0 (stayed clean)
- Cognitive complexity: well under threshold both ways (no SonarJS violation either state)
- APP mass: ~27 (three functions, two extra invocation hops) → ~20 (one function)
- McCabe: `survivesOrIsBorn` 2 → `isLiveNextGeneration` 3 (the `||` and `&&` now live in one function; total decision count across the file is conserved, two trivial functions removed)
- Elements: 3 functions → 1 function

**Tests**: 9/9 passing.

**Decision rationale**: Rule 2 (reveals intent) was the deciding factor since APP/McCabe were near-floor either way. The merged predicate reads as the canonical Conway statement ("born by reproduction, OR alive and surviving") with named constants preserving intent, eliminates the asymmetric signatures, and wins on Rule 4 (3→1 elements) without introducing any ESLint smell or breaking a test. Kept.

**Final-pass review**: The whole file reads as a clean linear pipeline (type → constants → key serialization → geometry → neighbor counting → survival rule → generation orchestration). No remaining clarity or simplicity nits; the sparse `Set<string>` representation is idiomatic for an unbounded grid and handles negative coordinates naturally.

Relevant file: `/home/experimenter/experiments/runs/2026-05-30_20-42-31_game-of-life-example-mapping_v6.4-metric-driven-refactor_opus-4-8-no-thinking/src/game-of-life.ts`

Done marker written to `experiment-done.txt`.
